contest.title: IAEPC Preliminary Contest (Codeforces Round 999, Div. 1 + Div. 2)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints checked. Every operation moves every stone to a distinct neighboring vertex; H1 asks only whether the target configuration is reachable.
The published editorial was consulted, but the H1/H2 section still displays "Tutorial is loading..." and the post says hints and code are WIP, so it supplied no usable proof.

Inspected maspy's accepted H1 source. It special-cases equal states, finds saturating neighbor matchings for both endpoints, builds the bipartite double cover with DSU, and checks both possible target-layer parities.
Used the H2 analysis to cross-check the equivalent component-count and bipartite-color conditions and the constructive sufficiency argument.
Inspected the accepted H2 construction that turns the H1 conditions into an explicit schedule, thereby confirming sufficiency rather than only necessity.

Independently proved the matching and double-cover invariants. Exhaustive enumeration of every simple graph with n <= 5 and every equal-cardinality endpoint pair matched exact configuration-graph reachability in all 262702 cases.

annotation.summary: Given an undirected graph and equally many stones in initial and target vertex sets, decide whether synchronized collision-free moves, where every stone must move to a neighbor each step, can transform the initial set into the target set.
annotation.constraints: t <= 1000; n <= 2000 and m <= min(n(n-1)/2, 10000) per test; sum n <= 2000 and sum m <= 10000; the two configurations contain the same number of stones.
annotation.core_idea: If the two configurations are identical, zero operations are valid and no mobility condition is needed. Otherwise, the first operation requires a matching that assigns every initially occupied vertex a distinct neighbor, and reversing the final operation gives the same requirement for the target set. Build the bipartite double cover D with vertices (v,0),(v,1) and cross-layer copies of every original edge. A walk of length k starting in layer 0 ends in layer p=k mod 2, and no stone can leave its connected component of D. Therefore, for one global p in {0,1}, every component of D must contain equally many initial stones in layer 0 and target stones in layer p. These conditions are also sufficient: the two endpoint matchings can be lifted into D, their alternating cycles removed, and their union extended to a forest on which the H2 construction routes all stones. Equivalently, a non-bipartite original component gives one double-cover component, while a bipartite component splits into two and enforces either preserved or swapped color-class counts; all bipartite components must agree on the same time parity.
annotation.complexity: O(m sqrt(n) + (n + m) alpha(n)) time and O(n + m) memory per test case using Hopcroft-Karp and DSU.
tricks: Accept identical configurations immediately because a zero-length sequence is legal.
Characterize whether a configuration can make one step by a matching saturating all occupied vertices.
Lift the graph into two parity layers so walk parity becomes ordinary connectivity.
Use one global operation-count parity across all connected components.
Interpret split versus connected double-cover components as the bipartite versus non-bipartite cases.

solution_variants.name: bipartite-double-cover-matching-criterion
solution_variants.summary: After special-casing equal states, find saturating neighbor matchings for the initial and target occupied sets. Build the two-layer double cover, then accept iff for p=0 or p=1 every double-cover component has equal initial-layer-0 and target-layer-p stone counts.
solution_variants.complexity: O(m sqrt(n) + (n + m) alpha(n)) time and O(n + m) memory.

tags.description: Duplicate every graph vertex into two parity layers and replace each original edge by cross-layer copies, using the resulting bipartite cover to encode even/odd walks and parity-sensitive connectivity.
tags.created_reason: The existing layered-graph and bipartite-graph tags do not identify the canonical parity double cover that is the decisive reachability model in both versions. Main-agent taxonomy review is required before accepting this candidate; add a WebUI translation if accepted.
tags.evidence: Reachability is characterized by connected-component balances between initial layer 0 and target layer p in the bipartite double cover.

For every nonzero solution, both endpoint configurations must admit an injective assignment from occupied vertices to distinct neighbors; two saturating bipartite matchings test exactly this condition.
All stones take the same number of steps, so one global parity p determines which double-cover layer contains every target vertex.
Stone counts are conserved independently inside every connected component of the double cover, and equality of these component balances is the central reachability test.
The two saturation checks can be expressed as unit-capacity max-flow instances, explaining the official flow tag, but the reviewed implementation uses bipartite matching directly.
